key_points: Force is a push or pull acting on an object that can change its motion or shape
Forces can change the speed, direction or shape of an object
Balanced forces are equal forces acting in opposite directions that do not change the motion of an object
Unbalanced forces cause a change in the state of motion of an object
Inertia is the tendency of an object to resist change in its state of rest or motion
Mass of an object is a measure of its inertia
Newton’s First Law states that an object remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by an unbalanced force
Momentum is defined as the product of mass and velocity of an object
Newton’s Second Law states that force equals mass multiplied by acceleration (F = ma)
Newton’s Third Law states that for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction
examples: A book resting on a table shows balanced forces
Pushing a stationary box causes motion due to unbalanced force
Passengers move forward when a bus stops suddenly due to inertia of motion
Passengers move backward when a bus starts suddenly due to inertia of rest
Recoil of a gun when a bullet is fired illustrates Newton’s Third Law
Rocket propulsion works on the principle of action and reaction
formulas: Impulse → Force × time = Change in momentum
exam_tips: Remember that inertia depends on mass of the object
Newton’s First Law is also called the Law of Inertia
Momentum increases with increase in mass or velocity
Action and reaction forces act on different objects but are equal in magnitude
Force, momentum and acceleration are vector quantities
